Q1.6.question_text: Based on Mr. G. Krishnan's insights from the international business workshop, discuss the Business Model Canvas for HTPL with reference to the information given in the case study and how the following elements of the model can streamline operations and uncover growth opportunities: 1. Key Partners, activities and resources 2. Customer relationships 3. Customer segments 4. Cost structure 5. Value proposition

Q1.7.question_text: Forward Hedge: Forward Rate ` 104/€, Amount required to be paid after 3-months (€ 15000 x 2) € 30,000. Compute the cost under Forward Hedge, Money Market Hedge, and Forward Hedge under LC Option, and recommend the best hedging strategy.

Q1.8.question_text: Computation of depreciation on plant and machinery (Block 15%) for the A.Y. 2026-27. WDV as on 1.4.2025 is ` 120 crore. New machinery and equipment acquired on 10.8.2025 including freight and installation charges ` 2.55 crore. Imported tea sachet forming machinery put to use 25.3.2026. Insurance receipts for loss of machinery in fire ` 1.40 crore. Compute the total depreciation for P.Y. 2025-26 including additional depreciation, and compute the actual cost of imported tea sachet forming machinery.

Q2.6.question_text: Roshan Joshi believes that the operational inefficiencies in Heritage Organic are a necessary part of the artisanal process. However, Geeta Sharma plans to eliminate them. Based on the information in the given scenario, explain which of the identified 'Non-Value-Added' activities can be eliminated and what will be its strategic implication?

Q2.7.question_text: Consequences of buy back of shares: (i) Consequences of buy back of shares in the hands of shareholder and its obligation - In case of buyback of shares by Heritage Organic, the sum of ` 150 per share paid by it for purchase of its own shares would be treated as dividend and taxable under the head 'Income from Other Sources' in the hands of shareholders. No deduction for expenses would be available against such dividend income. The consideration received on buy-back of shares will be taken as nil and the entire cost of acquisition of shares would be either long term or short-term capital loss based on period of holding of respective shareholder. The said long term or short-term capital loss is eligible for set-off and carry forward as per provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961. (ii) Consequences of buy back of shares in the hands of company and its obligation - There would be no tax implications in the hands of Heritage Organic on buy back of its shares. The company is required to deduct tax at source @10% under section 194. However, tax is not required to be deducted at source where shareholder is an individual, the dividend is paid by any mode other than cash and amount of deemed dividend does not exceed ` 10,000.

Q2.8.question_text: CA Gaurav should give following advice to the company regarding: Tax liability - An activity or transaction is considered as supply only when it is supply of goods or services or both for consideration in course or furtherance of business. However, Schedule I of the CGST Act, 2017 specifies exceptions where an activity or transaction is deemed to be a supply even without consideration. One such exception is gifts exceeding ` 50,000 in value in a financial year by an employer to an employee. Since in the given case, amount of gift does not exceed ` 50,000 per employee, gifting of home appliances to employees and workers does not amount to supply. ITC admissibility - Following ITC availed is inadmissible: (i) ITC of ` 150 Lakh is not admissible in the month of March 2024, since goods have not been received in said month. (ii) ITC of ` 2.70 lakh is not admissible in November 2023 since ITC on outdoor catering is blocked if the same is not used for making an outward supply of outdoor catering or as an element of a taxable composite/mixed supply. (iii) ITC on gifts to employees is inadmissible since ITC on goods disposed of by way of gift is blocked under section 17(5). Therefore, above credit will be reversed. Interest and penalty - Interest under section 50 is payable on amount of utilized ITC from the date of utilisation of such wrongly availed ITC (i.e. 18th April, 2024, earlier of GSTR-3B due date or filing date) till the date of reversal of such credit or payment of tax in respect of such amount @ 15% per annum. The wrongly availed ITC is deemed to be utilised to the extent the Electronic Credit Ledger balance falls below such ITC. Thus, interest is payable on ITC utilised, i.e. ` 42.60 lakh [(150 Lakh + ` 2.70 lakh + 9.90 lakh) - ` 120 lakh]. Penalty as per the provisions of section 74A, will be applicable.

Q3.6.question_text: HCL requires you to calculate "Deferred tax" arising on acquisition of SCL and the goodwill arising thereon.

Q3.7.question_text: HCL has proposed a new foreign investment which involves creation of a plant with an annual output of 1 million units. Taking into consideration the other inputs given in the case study above, you are requested to find out: (i) Annual Cash Flow after Tax (CFAT) under all the different scenarios of exchange rate. (ii) Expected value of CFAT assuming no repatriation of profits. (iii) Viability of the investment proposal on the basis of CFAT arrived under option (ii) assuming an initial investment of USD 25 million on plant and working capital with a required rate of return of 11% on investment. The CFAT will grow @ 3% per annum in perpetuity.

Q3.8.question_text: Decide the correctness of the views of the Tax Counsel and suggest the course of action to be pursued by the Company in respect of the Income-tax appeals preferred by HCL before the CIT(A) for the A.Y. 2023-24 and an appeal for the Assessment Year 2024-25 before the Appellate Tribunal.

Q4.6.question_text: Per Ind AS 2 'Inventories', most by-products as well as scrap or waste materials, by their nature, are immaterial. They are often measured at net realisable value and this value is deducted from the cost of the main product. [Question requiring calculation of value of stocks including NRV of by-product, cost of conversion for joint products MP1 and MP2, allocation of joint cost, and determination of value of closing stock of MP1, MP2 and raw material]

Q4.7.question_text: Discuss the considerations when accepting an engagement to audit a single financial statement or a specific element of a financial statement, and the considerations when planning and performing such an audit, with reference to SA 805, SA 800, SA 200 and other relevant SAs. Also discuss how R & Co. LLP should approach the engagement as statutory auditor of VHIL's complete financial statements while auditing a specific element.

Q4.8.question_text: Pricing strategy for automated tunnelling machinery in the African Market: Discuss the appropriate pricing strategy that VHIL should adopt while entering the African market, considering the mixed market research reports, competitive conditions, and the nature of the product.

Q_CaseStudy_Part15.question_text: Case Scenario: Avigo Airlines operational crisis, financial performance, bond refinancing, lease arrangement, and TDS survey issues. (i) Company has made payment of lease rent of ₹24 crore to PQR Fleet Services IFSC Limited located in Gujarat International Finance Tec-City for lease of an aircraft which commenced in June 2025. Neither tax has been deducted on lease rent paid during the year nor these payments have been reported in TDS returns filed by the company. (ii) Company has paid landing and parking charges of ₹12 crore to Airport Authority of India and ₹8 crore to private airport operators. The company has deducted tax @ 2% instead of 10% as the payments are in the nature of rent paid for use of space. (iii) Company has taken an engine on lease in financial year 2025-26 under an arrangement with a foreign company, a tax resident of Germany. Company deducted tax at source @ 10% on lease rental paid as per the provisions contained under DTAA between India and Germany. Chief Accountant informed that foreign company has no permanent establishment in India. However, revenue officers contended that in the absence of furnishing of PAN, TDS is to be deducted at a higher rate of 20% as per the relevant provisions of Income-tax Act, 1961. (iv) The Company allowed exemptions and deductions to its employees during financial year 2024-25 relevant to Assessment Year 2025-26 on account of various items.

Q5.6.question_text: How can Avigo Global Limited align and integrate all seven elements of the McKinsey's 7S framework to strengthen operational robustness, enhance competitive advantage and prevent a recurrence of operational crisis, while ensuring that each element reinforces the others to create a resilient, efficient and customer-focused airline?

Q5.7.question_text: In the given case, the company has decided to exercise the extension option relating to the original 6,000 sq. ft. lease. Avigo thus concludes at the end of year 5 that it is reasonably certain that it will exercise the extension option. Thus, the lease liability and right of use (ROU) asset for the original lease will be modified while the lease for the additional floor will be treated as separate lease in accordance with para 44 Ind AS 116. Calculate the lease liability at commencement date, measurement of RoU asset, depreciation of RoU asset till 31st March 2026, lease liability at the end of 5th year, reassessment of extension option on 31st March 2026, and pass the journal entry to recognise lease modification as on 1st April 2026.

Q5.8.question_text: Discuss the following TDS related issues: (i) Non deduction of tax at source on lease rent paid to PQR Fleet Services IFSC Ltd. - No tax was deductible on lease rent paid to PQR Fleet Services IFSC Limited, being a unit located in IFSC, GIFT City, engaged in aircraft leasing business and eligible for deduction under section 80LA in view of the CBDT Notification No. 65/2022 dated 16.06.2022 issued under section 197A(1F). (ii) Short deduction of tax at source on lease rent of engine paid to foreign company - The Company deducted tax at source @10% on engine lease rental paid to German company in accordance with the India-Germany DTAA. The non-resident payee does not have any Permanent Establishment in India and the applicable DTAA rate being more beneficial. Discuss with reference to the Supreme Court ruling in case of CIT (International Taxation) v. Air India Ltd. [2023] 456 ITR 139. (iii) Short deduction of tax at source on landing and parking charges - The company had relied on Supreme Court ruling in case of Japan Airlines Co. Ltd. v. CIT / CIT v. Singapore Airlines Ltd. (2015) 377 ITR 372, wherein the Apex Court held that the landing and parking charges are not merely for the 'use of the land' and thus tax is not deductible under section 194-I.
